data.title: Hong Kong triathletes hungry for more National Games joy after Oscar Coggins’ bronze
data.description:
After taking a “step in the right direction” in the National Games individual races, Hong Kong’s triathletes have targeted a significant leap forward in the mixed relay on Sunday.
In a “brutal” men’s event, Oscar Coggins claimed a redemptive bronze, the first major Games medal of his career.
Running on empty towards the end, Robin Elg eased off to conserve energy for the relay. Wong Tsz-to was 18th, as an unforgiving sun and exacting course left 20 of the 48 starters unable to finish.
Earlier on Saturday, Cade Wright was fourth in the women’s race, as only 16 of the 40-strong field survived the Central Harbourfront challenge. One of those, Hilda Choi Yan-yin, was disqualified soon after crossing the line for an in-race infringement.

Already detached from the lead group, Bailee Brown’s hopes expired when a staple punctured one of her tyres on the bike leg.
“She was very smart; rather than go hard to the wheel station and potentially crash, she decided to save herself for the relay,” Andrew Wright, the head coach, said. “Maybe it was a blessing in disguise.”
Huang Anqi of the China Public Security Sports Association won the women’s race in one hour, 59 minutes and 16 seconds. Men’s honours went to Henan’s imperious Fan Junjie in 1:47.47.
Wright said his emotions following the medal for Tokyo Olympian Coggins were evenly split between joy and relief.
“There was more pressure than for an Olympics or Asian Games, because the expectation was higher,” Wright said.
“It cost so much to put this on; so much effort has gone into it. The athletes and I had to do our part to show we’re competitive and our programme will be successful.
“Today was a step in the right direction.”
Coggins and Elg executed the pre-race plan of starting quickly to cut adrift a large rump of the field.
Nevertheless, Wright said he was “a bit worried” that his star pair had five rivals in the front group.
“I know the calibre of the run from those athletes,” he said. “But the boys kept attacking on the bike and they tore the legs off some of the guys for the run.”
An unhappy soul when he withdrew from the individual Asian Games race in 2023, the revitalised Coggins held firm on the run as other contenders ran out of gas.
“I’m super-happy – we executed our plan really well; we were aggressive in making it only a small group of athletes competing for medals out of the swim,” said Coggins, adding that the “amazing” home support had dragged him around a “really hard last run lap”.
Coggins, who finished in 1:49.21, 57 seconds behind Liaoning’s Chen Qi in second, had Elg on his tail for the opening two 2.5km (1.55-mile) run laps, but paid no attention to the identity of his nearest rival. The race was tough enough already.
He was aiming for another “hard and aggressive” start to the relay. “Only Hong Kong and Shandong had two athletes in the front pack,” Coggins said. “We showed we’re a very strong team today.”

Wright felt Elg would have finished fourth had he not listened to instructions to back off once his podium chance had gone.
Acknowledging he was “completely flat” as he spoke shortly after finishing, the visibly shattered Elg said: “It was a brutal race. The first two [run] laps, I was on Oscar’s heels; on the third, I knew he was having a better day than me.
“I expected to fight for the podium. I have no excuses. The guys in front of me did an insane job.”
Elg said two years of working on how to recover in time for relays would equip him for Sunday.
“We’re a hard team to beat and we need to expect nothing less than gold,” he said.

data.title: Why first Simandou iron ore shipment to China marks a global milestone
data.description:
The launch of Guinea’s US$20 billion Simandou iron ore project marks a globally significant milestone.
The event on Tuesday at Guinea’s Morebaya port, attended by Chinese and regional African leaders, saw the departure of the first shipment of ore from the Simandou project – a major feat after nearly three decades of development.
The Chinese delegation was led by Vice-Premier Liu Guozhong, who underscored the importance Beijing has attached to securing high-grade ore for decarbonising the national steel industry and diversifying away from Australian supplies.
Guinea’s Simandou 2040 plan aims to use mining revenues to develop infrastructure, agriculture and education in the West African country, while also signalling the mine’s immediate global importance.
Most extracts from Simandou – the world’s largest known untapped deposit of high-grade iron ore – are expected to be shipped to China, given the heavy investments by Chinese firms in the project. But the majority Chinese stake is a key concern for both the Guinea government and British-Australian mining giant Rio Tinto regarding future market stability, which has created an unusual alliance.
Guinean officials are aware of the possibility that Chinese firms would use the massive influx of high-grade ore to suppress global iron ore prices, but have pledged to actively collaborate with Rio Tinto to leverage the mine’s premium product and Rio Tinto’s market expertise to maintain stable high prices for the ore.
The Guinean minister of mines and geology, Bouna Sylla, said the country was monitoring the formation of China Mineral Resources Group (CMRG), which aims to centralise Chinese iron ore imports.
“We are closely following that development because it tells how we must engage in informal discussions with Rio Tinto about how we can work together to maintain a high iron ore price,” said Sylla, who is also a member of the Simandou Strategic Committee that monitors the project for the government.

As Rio Tinto is a pure miner, Guinea is “often closer to Rio Tinto in the desire to maintain a high iron ore price”, he added, referencing the diverse interests of the Chinese stakeholders, which also include processing and steelmaking.
The project is expected to ramp up production over 30 months to reach its full commercial capacity of 120 million tonnes annually, a volume that will position Guinea among the top global iron ore exporters.
A Rio Tinto executive who requested anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter acknowledged the market reality that most global ore went to China, and said that since China Baowu Steel Group was a major partner for Simandou, “a lot of this ore will go to China, there is no doubt”.
However, the executive said that Rio Tinto maintained market flexibility, saying: “Rio is not beholden to that and has the ability to sell to any market anywhere, so we will continue to explore those other markets.”
Rio Tinto and the Aluminium Corporation of China (Chinalco) own blocks 3 and 4 of Simandou under a consortium called Rio Tinto Simfer, while blocks 1 and 2 are owned by the Winning Consortium Simandou (WCS), whose key partners include Singapore’s Winning International Group, as well as China’s Weiqiao Aluminium and Baowu Resources. The Guinean government holds a 15 per cent stake in both, with an 18 million tonne share per year.

Crucially, to overcome delays caused by a lack of infrastructure, Simfer and WCS jointly funded and built the shared 650km (404-mile) railway and port of Morebaya infrastructure.
Sylla described the Simandou situation as “extremely complex”, noting that Guinea’s interests sometimes aligned with Rio Tinto, sometimes with Baowu, and other times with WCS because “WCS is a logistics specialist”.
Sylla also highlighted a complementarity between Simandou and Australian Pilbara ore. Simandou’s high-grade deposit targets the premium 65 per cent iron content blend market, making it comparable only to Brazil’s Vale, especially the Carajás deposits.
He said this gave Rio Tinto the unique opportunity to offer both products and sell the premium ore not only to China but also to European and Middle Eastern steel mills.
But the Simandou project is widely dubbed a potential Pilbara killer, a reference to its long-term threat to Western Australia’s market dominance.
According to Sylla, this superior quality is critical to China’s “green steel” industry and gives Rio Tinto the “unique opportunity to offer both products” globally, as most of Australian Pilbara ore typically focuses on the larger 62 per cent blend market.
Hu Wangming, the chairman of China Baowu, described Simandou as “a significant milestone” in the history of the global mining industry.
In a statement on Tuesday, Hu said that “the stable supply of Simandou’s premium iron ore resources will provide a solid foundation of low-carbon raw materials for the development of China’s steel industry and the global steel sector”.
Similarly, Chris Aitchison, general director of Rio Tinto Simfer, said that emerging steelmaking technologies required high-grade ore and Simandou’s quality allowed for reduced energy input in the process, thereby lowering carbon dioxide emissions.
“So, there is a direct correlation between the benefits of sourcing Simandou ore and people being able to use that ore in their new steelmaking technology processes.” So, it is a direct enabler; it is very important for China to achieve their goals,” Aitchison said.

Beijing-based lawyer Kai Xue noted that China’s shift from infrastructure to a hi-tech economy was reducing its demand for steel and iron ore. As China produced 54 per cent of the world’s steel in 2023, this transition signals a long-term contraction for the global iron ore market.
“With supply rising and demand falling, someone will inevitably be pushed out and that someone is likely to be the Pilbara region of Western Australia. The reason is geopolitical,” he said of Simandou’s entry.
Xue argued that Australia’s Pilbara region was likely to be displaced due to geopolitical factors, specifically Australia’s 2021 entry into the Aukus security pact with the United States and United Kingdom, focused on acquiring nuclear-powered submarines to boost its military standing in the Indo-Pacific.
This move prompted China to diversify its supply chains, unblocking the long-stalled Simandou project in Guinea.
Initiated by Rio Tinto in 1997, the project experienced many delays due to legal and political challenges before advancing with the partnerships involving Rio Tinto, Chinese firms and the Guinean government. Final construction started in 2022.
“The first shipment marks the moment Australia’s strategic choices began to undermine its own resource economy,” Xue said.

data.title: Angelica Lee Sinje evokes the ‘liberating happiness’ of childhood in her Hong Kong art show
data.description:
Angelica Lee Sinje is a woman of many talents.
The Malaysia-born actress and pop singer, who starred alongside Shu Qi in the recent Netflix revenge horror, The Resurrected, showed her skill on the other side of the camera in 2023 when she co-produced the critically acclaimed Malaysian crime drama Abang Adik.
But in a quiet gallery in Hong Kong’s Sheung Wan neighbourhood, she revealed yet another, lesser-known talent: for painting.
Her solo exhibition at 13a New Street Art Gallery, called “Childtopia”, could not be more different in mood to The Eye, the 2002 horror for which she won various best actress awards, or The Resurrected, in which she plays a grieving and vengeful mother.

Instead, her paintings serve up a dose of warm, sickly-sweet nostalgia. The Chinese title of the exhibition, “Tong But Lut”, is named after the chewy Cantonese glutinous rice dessert.
The collection of more than 20 paintings can be seen as intimate portals into the inner world of the mother of three, weaving together personal reflections with a universal longing for the innocence of youth.
“The surface of the tong but lut rice balls is coated with a sticky peanut powder that cannot be washed away, symbolising the innocence that forever resides in our hearts,” Lee says. “I want to send forward a sense of purity, a childlike wonder in a life that we know to be so busy and complicated.”
She adds that she uses circular canvases, echoing the shape of the dessert, to evoke a sense of reunion, completeness and the cyclical nature of memory.
Each painting resembles the round face of a small child, embedded with varying colours and themes to describe a particular emotion or a specific memory for Lee.

One piece is an intimate portrait of her infant son, asleep with a dummy. Another, more abstract, painting tells the story of a boy’s magical transformation into a fish as he leaps into the ocean.
“Another element I wanted to focus on was the impact that nature had on me as a child,” she says.
Lee grew up in Alor Setar, a city north of Penang on the west coast of Peninsular Malaysia. “My family lived in the countryside, and I grew up running in the field, playing in the mud, climbing trees. It was freedom in every sense of the word,” she says.
“I wanted to capture that feeling, that unrestrained, liberating happiness. I feel that same joy in painting. It brings a spirit of wildness to my heart that nothing else can.”
Even the names of the artworks are meant to provoke untethered delight.
Titles such as Pineapple Bun, Ginger Milk Curd and Rice Cake evoke the indelible sensory imprint of childhood comfort foods, while also serving as a heartfelt nod to Hong Kong’s rich culture, which Lee became intimately connected with during her years living in the city from 2001 to 2007.

This connection sparked a playful idea. “Maybe I should do a series for every city I visit and name them after other local sweets,” she jokes.
Beyond the new paintings, the exhibition delves deeper into Lee’s personal and artistic history.
It includes four pieces from her 2023 Taipei solo exhibition, “Beyond Silence”, that are in marked contrast to the childlike new paintings. These are large canvases that she laboriously covered in fine, repetitive patterns reminiscent of Yayoi Kusama’s paintings. There are also three installation and mixed-media works connected to Lee’s family.
Two special audio installations offer an intimate glimpse into her world. In one, Lee herself narrates the story of her grandmother, Liu Yay Ue, revealing the roots of her creative awakening. In another, her son recites nostalgic Cantonese advertising jingles.
The opening ceremony for “Childtopia” was attended by some of Lee’s high-profile friends and peers, including Sylvia Chang, Gigi Leung and Jan Lamb.
“Childtopia” – Sinje Lee Solo Exhibition, 13a New Street Art Gallery, 13a New Street, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong, November 7-30, 2025. Wednesdays to Sundays, 12pm-7pm.

data.title: China duo caught inscribing words like ‘success’, ‘good luck’ at ancient sites to change fate
data.description:
Two men caught inscribing positive words and their names on rocks at 35 historical sites on China’s Mount Tai in a single day claim they did so to “change their fate”.
The case gained national attention on November 7, when China’s Supreme People’s Court released details of it to mark the first anniversary of the revised Law of the People’s Republic of China on Cultural Relics Protection.
The move was also aimed at raising public awareness about cultural heritage preservation.
Two men are from Heze in northern China’s Shandong province.

One of them, Zhang, only completed primary school and the other, Li, is illiterate.
They became convinced that writing their names and phrases like “success”, “good luck” and “blessings” on the sacred Taoist mountain of Mount Tai could change their fate.
Both men had previously received administrative penalties for defrauding others using feudal superstitions.
Mount Tai, in Tai’an City, Shandong province, is a Unesco World Cultural Heritage site and is recognised as the easternmost of China’s Five Sacred Mountains.

Its ancient architecture and stone inscriptions carry significant historical, artistic and scientific value.
On July 18, 2021, the duo took red marker pens to the Red Gate of Mount Tai and proceeded to deface multiple cultural relics.
In a single day, they defaced a total of 35 cultural relics, six of which were under national-level protection and 29 under provincial-level care.
The graffiti mainly consisted of phrases seeking blessings or good fortune, such as “Open the Celestial Base”, along with their own names and “Made it” or “Time to Rise”.
The men claimed they were praying for their families’ safety and their children’s academic success.
They also admitted that they had watched short videos online which claimed that writing one’s name on a stone pillar and having others read it aloud could bring good luck.
Believing that Mount Tai was imbued with spiritual energy, they became convinced it would bring them even greater blessings.
The Taishan District People’s Court found Zhang and Li guilty of intentionally damaging cultural relics, sentencing Zhang to one year in prison with two years' probation and a fine of 2,000 yuan (US$280), while Li was fined 1,500 yuan.
Both men were also ordered to make a public apology and pay 70,588 yuan (US$9,900) in ecological damage compensation.
Considering their financial situations, the court allowed them to fulfil the compensation through public service labour.

Zhang must complete 2,672 hours of community service within two years and Li must complete 151 hours within six months.
It is a common practice at scenic spots for people to carve their names and messages, often driven by superstitions or the belief that such acts will help fulfil wishes.
The case sparked sarcasm and humour on mainland social media.
One person said: “Well, it worked, he really changed his fate. From a hard life to a prison life.”
While another said: “Mount Tai really is spiritual. His wish came true. Time to reflect and reform in jail.”

data.title: Hongkongers living in mainland China urged to vote in coming Legco election
data.description:
Hongkongers living in mainland China have been urged to vote in the coming Legislative Council election, with authorities planning to set up a special polling station at the newly opened airport terminal.
The Hong Kong government’s Beijing office on Saturday held a briefing on election arrangements and the role of the legislature in Beijing, encouraging voters on the mainland to return to the city to cast their votes.
Amy Yuen Wai-yin, acting director of the office, said the airport polling station would offer more convenience to electors living across the border.
“In the previous District Council election, the near-boundary polling stations were located in Sheung Shui, but some friends told me they were not travelling back through land border crossings,” she said.
“But this time, there will also be extra polling stations at the airport, which makes it more convenient for everyone.
“The polling time has also been extended and will run from 7.30am to 11.30pm. I hope that such arrangements will allow everyone who wants to vote to cast their ballots.”
Speaking separately on Saturday, Secretary for Transport and Logistics Mable Chan revealed that a new airport’s polling station would be located at Terminal 2, which opened in September.
“Other than facilitating the voting by returning residents, employees who work at the airport, such as Airport Authority staff and members of airlines, can also vote here,” she said.
Hong Kong will elect 90 new Legco members on December 7.
Among them, 20 will be directly elected by residents from 10 geographical constituencies. Thirty and 40 will come from functional and the Election Committee constituencies, respectively, where lawmakers will be picked by their respective small circles.
As part of the government’s initiative to boost the turnout rate at the coming Legco election, polling stations will be set up at the airport and the passenger clearance building for the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge, alongside near-boundary stations in Sheung Shui to help residents living across the border vote.
This marks an expansion from a similar measure in the 2023 District Council election, where four near-boundary polling stations were established.
As a result, 9,991 out of nearly 1.2 million people who voted in the 2023 poll used these stations.
Meanwhile, authorities will also introduce designated polling stations for civil servants, members of disciplined forces, Hospital Authority staff and ethnic minorities. Outreach stations will also be set up in residential care homes for the elderly and those for people with disabilities.
Voters will need to make prior registrations to use these polling stations, as it involves changes from the original ones allocated to them.
In 2021, the turnout rate in the first Legco election after Beijing’s “patriots-only” electoral overhaul in Hong Kong dipped to a historical low of 30.2 per cent.
In an effort to drive participation this year, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu has written to the city’s more than 170,000 civil servants, encouraging them to vote.
The private sector has also echoed the calls by authorities, laying out various measures to facilitate employees’ voting, including offering reimbursement for traffic expenses, additional half-day paid leave and flexible work arrangements.
Also on Saturday, Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung shared on social media that he had visited members of various disciplined forces and the Auxiliary Medical Service over the past two days to encourage them to cast their votes.
“The disciplined forces must set an example and be the first to cast their ballots, leading the public to vote together and build a better future for Hong Kong,” he said.

data.title: 6 dead, 17 missing after landslide hits Indonesia’s Central Java: state media
data.description:
A landslide in Indonesia’s Central Java killed six people with 17 missing, state news agency Antara reported on Saturday, after prolonged, heavy rains.
The landslide on Thursday in the city of Cilacap buried a dozen houses in Cibeunying village, Antara said, citing disaster official Budi Irawan.
“We have found three more bodies, leaving only 17 more to be found. We have been working to the best of our ability,” Budi, a deputy at Indonesia’s disaster management agency, was quoted as saying.
The agency had previously found three bodies.

Budi said the location was challenging for rescuers as victims were buried 3 metres to 8 metres (10 feet to 25 feet) deep.
The Southeast Asian nation’s wet season started in September and will last until April, the weather agency says, bringing a higher risk of floods and extreme rainfall in many areas.
In January, a landslide triggered by torrential rain in another Central Java city, Pekalongan, killed at least 25 people.

data.title: 2 Hong Kong men arrested over vandalising Legco election campaign posters
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested two men for allegedly vandalising campaign materials for the coming Legislative Council election.
Police said on Saturday that officers found three vandalised election campaign posters hanging from the Tong Mi Road footbridge in Mong Kok on Thursday.
After an investigation, officers arrested two local men, aged 29 and 34, in Mong Kok on Friday on suspicion of criminal damage, the force said.
Police said the two suspects were being detained as part of the investigation.
“Police reiterate that the force will adopt a zero-tolerance attitude towards any attempt or intention to interfere with or disrupt the Legislative Council election, and make every effort to conduct investigations,” Inspector Yip Ho-lam said.
“Police will also do their utmost to enforce the law strictly and work closely with the public to ensure that residents can vote safely in the Legislative Council election on December 7.”
It is an offence for a person who destroys or damages any property belonging to another without lawful excuse, and carries a maximum penalty of 10 years in prison.
Police conducted multiple law enforcement actions in recent weeks to crack down on vandalism of election campaign materials.
Three men were arrested last weekend as part of separate cases for allegedly damaging election campaign banners.
On Friday, the Independent Commission Against Corruption also arrested two men and a woman for allegedly reposting online content inciting others to abstain from voting or to cast invalid ballots in the election.
The coming election will see 51 candidates competing for the 20 seats in the 10 geographical constituencies where residents will cast their ballots.
Another 60 are vying for 30 seats in the functional constituencies, and the remaining 50 for 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency. These seats are decided by sector-specific voters.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Cade Wright delivers coming-of-age National Games performance
data.description:
Cade Wright said she “loved every second” of her unexpected challenge for the National Games podium, after the Hongkonger narrowly missed out on a medal in an attritional women’s triathlon on Central Harbourfront.
The 22-year-old Wright finished fourth and delivered what she called an “outstanding, career-best swim” on the way to a time of two hours, one minute and 25 seconds, a shade more than two minutes behind winner Huang Anqi.
She acknowledged she had not been expecting to feature at the front, given she had one eye on Sunday’s mixed relay.
“I was aiming for top 10 … I’d spoken with my coach [Andrew Wright] about keeping something in reserve, because the relay is where we have medal potential,” she said.
“But it was very exciting. I was loving every second of being in medal contention.”

She entered the closing run leg in a small pack hunting down long-time leader Lin Xinyu, who would claim silver.
“My run form’s been improving a lot, but some of the girls had a better day than me,” Wright said. “I’m so chuffed with fourth. I never thought I would be remotely competing.”
Andrew Wright said he did not share his athlete’s surprise.
“One thing lacking in a lot of our athletes is self-belief,” he said. “I can see the talent in these guys.”
The coach said that when Wright completes her degree in mathematics at Edinburgh University “we will see her full talent shining through”.
“Everyone else in that field has been a full-time athlete since a young age,” he said. “Cade still isn’t; she’s in university doing a very hard course.”
Cade Wright said she had gained an appreciation of what was possible.
“We have a great coach and training environment, and so much support, so I hope for better results in major Games,” said Wright, whose parents travelled from Edinburgh to watch her. “I’m going to find them and give them a hug.”
After going in under the radar on Saturday, Wright will compete under medal expectation on Sunday. “Pressure is a privilege,” she said. “There will be pressure, but I really enjoy what I do.”

data.title: 18 injured in double-decker bus crash at stop near Hong Kong airport
data.description:
A double-decker bus crashed at a stop near Hong Kong airport on Saturday, leaving at least 18 people injured.
Police said they had received a report from the driver of a Citybus vehicle at 3.16pm that it had crashed into a planter by a bus stop close to the airport’s Terminal One.
“Eighteen people have been injured, with some sent to Princess Margaret Hospital and others sent to North Lantau Hospital. It is unclear if the driver is among the injured,” a spokesman said.
The spokesman added that the bus stop was at 1 Cheong Hong Road, with the vehicle heading in the direction of the airport.
The bus was part of Citybus’ A12 service, which goes from Siu Sai Wan to the airport.

data.title: Pluribus, AppleTV+’s dystopian sci-fi, makes the end of the world look happier than ever
data.description:
The end of the world is not usually so cheery.
In Pluribus, the new Apple TV+ series from Breaking Bad creator Vince Gilligan, scientists encounter a mysterious repeating signal coming from outer space.
That discovery leads researchers working for the United States military to encode a sort of virus that inevitably gets set loose and, after brief periods of mass seizures, leads to a psychic harmony among every human on Earth, who now operate as an “us”.
The thing is, they are really very nice. They offer pooled resources, true equality and, above all else, contentment.
For Carol Sturka (Rhea Seehorn), a grumpy romantasy writer who is immune and surrounded by smiling altruists at her home in Albuquerque, New Mexico, things could not be worse.

Typically, post-apocalyptic and dystopian speculative fiction narratives offer worst-case scenarios. Think Fallout, 28 Years Later and The Last of Us.
Pluribus, though, forces its viewers to genuinely consider whether giving in to this new world would be so bad after all.
“If this is a vision of absolute happiness in the calmest, most secure way possible, but it means losing being what is distinctively you, is it worth it? That’s obviously the tension that we’re trying to figure out,” Gordon Smith, the show’s executive producer, says.
“And we don’t know.”
Smith says Pluribus – Latin for “of many” – stands apart from other The Body Snatcher-esque projects because it does not have Cold War concerns. Those films released from the 1950s to the 1980s, as well as countless episodes of The Twilight Zone, were energised by fears of nuclear war and communism.
Pluribus feels radical because Carol’s enemies genuinely seem to care. They let animals out of zoos. They provide healthcare for everyone. They are vegetarians and they refuse to kill any living being, even a fly. Is the modern world just too cynical to believe ending it could actually be paradise?
In fact, when Carol, in the second episode, meets with the other English speakers who have not yet “joined” with the others, she is in the minority. One man is charmed that these people will seemingly cater to his every need. Another woman maintains that her son is still very much her child, even though he now shares a hivemind.

Carol is, in a way, the ugly American – loud, abrasive and willing to break up the party with her harsh truths.
“There’s 7 billion people on one side,” Smith says, noting that the writers felt as if they had to make it feel as though there was “some beauty on the other side” so Carol actually has some hard decisions to make.
The series is something of a Rorschach test, Smith says. It is not trying to directly evoke the news cycle, even though some viewers have already regarded it as a show about artificial intelligence.
The joined are a little bit like ChatGPT come to life with their all-consuming knowledge – every person on Earth now has the skills and memories of everyone else – but without the spuriousness of a learned language model. The eeriest thing about them is that you can see hints of the people they were before this all happened, the pain they have pushed down.
“If we’re feeling didactic or like we’ve got a lesson, then we’re steering into the wrong waters,” Smith says. “We want to make sure this is a human scale.”
But at the same time, it is hard to watch Pluribus and not think about the times in which we live, even if the metaphor is not as obvious as it is in, say, The Running Man or The Handmaid’s Tale, where the rise of fascism is on the menu.
These days, it feels as if polarisation is the language of humanity, and not in a good way. One just needs to log on to X, formerly known as Twitter, to see that.
Carol, in some ways, represents our fractured world with her fury. At the same time, we must root for her. That is in part because if she simply gave up, that would not make for very entertaining television.
More crucially, however, it is because our ability to feel is what makes us human, and is not hanging on to humanity what all our dystopias are about, anyway?
